coredata.dc:title: Construction of CdTe@γ-CD@RBD nanoprobe for Fe3+-sensing based on FRET mechanism in human serum
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Wenlu
Hu, Zhongfei
Shi, Cai
Xu, Ruoqian
Zhao, Yiming
Ding, Yujie
coredata.dc:description: A Fe3+ optical sensor (CdTe@γ-CD@RBD) has been developed by using gamma-cyclodextrin (γ-CD) as a bridge to link CdTe quantum dots (QDs) and a Rhodamine B derivative (RBD). The RBD molecule can enter the cavity of the γ-CD anchored onto the surfaces of the QDs. In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process from QDs to RBD will be initiated, rendering the nanoprobe to display a response to Fe3+. The degree of fluorescence quenching presented a satisfactory linearity between 10 and 60 μΜ with the incremental concentrations of Fe3+, and the calculated limit of detection was 2.51 μΜ. Through sample pretreatment procedures, the probe has been used in the determination of Fe3+ in human serum. The average recoveries in the spiking levels are ranged from 98.60 % to 107.20 % with a relative standard deviation of around 1.43 %-2.96 %. This finding leads to a method for fluorescent detection of Fe3+ with high sensitivity and exceptional selectivity. We believe that this study can give a new insight into the rational design and application of FRET-based nanoprobes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Assembly nanomaterials
Fluorescence
Probe
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Fe3+ and interference experiment
Analysis of Fe3+ in human serum samples
Preparation and characterization of the CdTe QDs, RBD and the composites
Optimize the amounts of γ-CD and RBD
The FRET activity of the probe
The sensitivity and selectivity of CdTe@γ-CD@RBD to Fe3+

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Fund in Anhui province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the overseas visiting and research project for outstanding young talents of colleges and universities in Anhui province ( gxgwfx2019043 ), undergraduate scientific research project of Anhui polytechnic university in 2022 (2022DZ25) and College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Fund in Anhui province ( S201910363194 , S202110363275 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM images of (a) CdTe QDs and (b) CdTe@γ-CD@RBD, (c)SEM, and (d) XRD characterizations of CdTe QDs, The line graph at the bottom is the standard data of sphalerite cubic phase CdTe (JCPDS 15–0770).
Infrared spectra characterizations of CdTe QDs (black), γ-CD (red), CdTe@γ-CD (blue), CdTe@γ-CD @RBD (green) and RBD (purple).

Effect of the amounts of γ-CD (a) and RBD (b) on the fluorescence quenching degree (I0-I)/I0 of 0.5 mg mL−1 CdTe@γ-CD@RBD composite at 600 nm, respectively.（I0 is the initial fluorescence intensity, I is the fluorescence intensity with 50 μM of Fe3+）.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of RBD with (black line) or without (green line) Fe3+ and fluorescence emission spectrum of 0.3 mg mL−1 ethanol solution of CdTe QDs with the excitation at 290 nm (red line). (b) Time resolved fluorescence decay spectra of CdTe@γ-CD@RBD solution recorded at 600 nm before (black line) and after (red line) addition of Fe3+.

(a) the UV–vis absorption spectra of 0.5 mg mL−1 CdTe@γ-CD@RBD solution with 0–60 μM Fe3+ in PBS solution (pH 7.4), (b) the linear relationship between UV–vis absorption intensity at 559 nm and Fe3+concentration (A is the UV–vis absorption intensity with different Concentrations of Fe3+), (c) the fluorescence spectra of 0.5 mg mL−1 CdTe@γ-CD@RBD with 0–60 μM Fe3+ in PBS solution (pH 7.4) with the excitation at 290 nm, (d) the linear relationship between the napierian logarithm of fluorescence quenching degree at 600 nm and Fe3+concentration（I0 is the initial fluorescence intensity, I is the fluorescence intensity with different concentrations of Fe3+).

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra, (b) the histogram of fluorescence quenching degree, of 0.6 mg mL−1 CdTe@γ-CD@RBD solution before or after addition of 50 μM Fe3+, 250 μM of other metal cations (Cd2+, K+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, Pb2+, Hg2+) and Hg2+, respectively (I is the fluorescence emission intensity with Fe3+, I0 is the initial fluorescence emission intensity).
Mechanism of FRET generation in CdTe@γ-CD@RBD composite.
The schematic diagram for the synthesis of CdTe@γ-CD@RBD composite.
The mechanism of RBD in the presence of Fe3+ and the FRET between CdTe QDs and RBD with Fe3+ under excitation at 290 nm wavelength.
Spiked recovery results for Fe3+ sensing employing the assembly materials in human serum.
A comparison of the proposed sensor with previous references for Fe3+ detection.

row.entry:
Bozkurt et al. (2015) [30]
Sun et al.(2022)[17]

head.ce:title: Construction of CdTe@γ-CD@RBD nanoprobe for Fe3+-sensing based on FRET mechanism in human serum

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anhui Polytechnic University, Wuhu, Anhui 241000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A Fe3+ optical sensor (CdTe@γ-CD@RBD) has been developed by using gamma-cyclodextrin (γ-CD) as a bridge to link CdTe quantum dots (QDs) and a Rhodamine B derivative (RBD). The RBD molecule can enter the cavity of the γ-CD anchored onto the surfaces of the QDs. In the presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process from QDs to RBD will be initiated, rendering the nanoprobe to display a response to Fe3+. The degree of fluorescence quenching presented a satisfactory linearity between 10 and 60 μΜ with the incremental concentrations of Fe3+, and the calculated limit of detection was 2.51 μΜ. Through sample pretreatment procedures, the probe has been used in the determination of Fe3+ in human serum. The average recoveries in the spiking levels are ranged from 98.60 % to 107.20 % with a relative standard deviation of around 1.43 %-2.96 %. This finding leads to a method for fluorescent detection of Fe3+ with high sensitivity and exceptional selectivity. We believe that this study can give a new insight into the rational design and application of FRET-based nanoprobes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ plays an indispensable role in many pathological processes, because it can oxidize proteins and other cellular components, resulting in certain toxicity. When an excess of Fe3+ is present in an organism, it increases the incidence of certain cancers and renal, hepatic, cardiac and pancreatic dysfunction. When the organism is deficient in Fe3+, diseases such as anemia or respiratory system disorders will be induced [1–3]. Therefore, the detection of Fe3+ is of great significance in biological environment. In order to achieve high sensitivity and selectivity, many fluorescent probes are constructed through complex compounds or specific chemical reactions [4–6]. Therefore, They must be used in organic solvents or at least mixtures of organic solvents/water, restricting their biological applications [7,8].

Fluorescent composites are formed by optimizing the combination of fluorescent materials with different properties by using advanced assembly technology. At present, many relevant materials have been constructed and then applied in analytical fields. Majumdar group [9] proposed a porous silsesquioxane cage and porphyrin nanocomposites applied in sensing and adsorption for heavy metals and anions. Wang et al [10] designed a fluorescent metal–organic framework for food real-time visual monitoring. Zhang et al [11] used fluorescent nanocomposites based on gold nanoclusters for metal ion detection and white light emitting diodes.

Recently, more and more fluorescent composite materials are used in optical sensors to detect biomacromolecule, small molecules, ions and so on based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) principle [12–14]. FRET is an energy transfer process between two fluorescent molecules in close proximity. Lei team [15] employed a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on g-CNQDs@Zn-MOF for the sensitive detection of riboflavin via FRET. Dewangan et al [16] applied N-doped carbon quantum dot-MnO2 nanowire FRET Pairs to detect cholesterol, glutathione, acetylcholinesterase and chlorpyrifos.

At the same time, the application of FRET for Fe3+ sensing has also been reported by some works. Hong group [17] assembled a FRET-based dual fluorescent probe with nitrogen doped carbon dots as the energy donor for selective detection of Fe3+. Zhang et al. [18] used one-pot method to construct carbon dots-based FRET material for Fe3+-sensing. In addition, a highly selective and sensitive FRET ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor has also been developed for detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution. The sensor consisted of rhodamine 6G moieties covalently coupled onto the surface of carbon dots. It worked based on a Fe3+-triggered FRET process between carbon dots and ring-opened rhodamine 6G [19].

Based on the aforementioned work, our research group designed a novel QDs-based fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection in aqueous solution. As shown in Scheme 1 , the sensor uses CdTe QDs as the energy donor and RBD as the energy acceptor. Gamma-cyclodextrin (γ-CD), a cyclic polysaccharide molecule containing glucose units with larger cavity diameter, serves as a linker [8]. It has both interior hydrophobic and external hydrophilic environment, which can make guest molecules fully or partially embedded in the cavity to form a stable assembly material. Therefore, γ-CD can act as a hub for the interconnection of CdTe QDs and RBD to avoid the interference of the complex external environment during the assembly of fluorescent probes. The QDs have a strong fluorescence emission peak at 600 nm under 290 nm excitation light. There is no obvious absorption peak from RBD in the absence of Fe3+. After Fe3+ adding, a wide ultraviolet absorption appears at 559 nm which indicates that there is a strong interaction between RBD and Fe3+. The fluorescence emission of QDs and the UV absorption of RBD-Fe3+ partially overlap with each other, and then the emission peak is weakened, which can be used to Fe3+-sensing.

All raw materials were purchased and used directly. Rhodamine B base (98 %), phosphorus oxychloride (POCl3, 98 %), diethylenetriamine (99 %), cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2.5H2O, 99 %), trisodium citrate (99 %), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 98 %), sodium tellurite (Na2TeO3, 99.8 %), sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 99 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.7 %), N-Hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS, 99 %), 3-aminophenylboronic acid (APBA, 99 %), 1-ethyl 3(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 99 %), and γ-CD (98 %) were purchased directly from Discovery Reagent online. Human serum was provided by the First People's Hospital of Wuhu City, Anhui province. The other reagents used directly were analytical grade.

The morphology of the QDs was characterized by TEM and HRTEM (USA-FEL-Talos-F200S) with a 200 kV accelerating voltage. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained by using an XRD-6000 diffractometer (CuKα radiation) provided by Jingong Instrument Technology Co., ltd (Suzhou, China). The scan rate was 0.05°/s with the 2-theta angle range of 10–80°. Infrared spectra were performed using a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer from Bruker, Germany, with a wavelength range of 4000–500 cm−1. The UV-2550 spectrum analyzer provided by Bidatech (USA) is used to test the UV–vis absorption spectrum. Fluorescence spectra were measured using an FT-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer provided by Tianmei Scientific Instruments Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). The time-resolved fluorescence decay was obtained with a high resolution Edinburgh Instrument FLS1000 spectrofluorometer with a 320 nm laser as the excitation source.

RBD was prepared according to the procedure described previously [8,20]. 1.0 g Rhodamine B base in 10 mL 1,2 dichloromethane was stirred, and then 0.6 mL POCl3 was joined drop by drop. The solution was refluxed for 3 h and evaporated to get rhodamine B acid chloride. Then the acquisition was dissolved in 80 mL acetonitrile, and then added by inches to a solution with 5 mL diethylenetriamine (in 25 mL acetonitrile under an ice bath). The mixture was stirred overnight. Solvent was removed and then the residue was washed with 20 mL water for four times. In the last, the solid was purified by column chromatography (CH2Cl2: CH3OH = 10: 1, v/v). The first pink band was collected to afford 0.198 mg product.

The QDs were synthesized through a one-pot synthetic process [21]. 0.11 g of CdCl2·2.5H2O and 0.20 g of trisodium citrate were together added in 60 mL of water, followed by addition of 52 μL of MPA. The pH was adjusted to 10.5 before accretion of 0.1 mmol Na2TeO3 and 37 mg NaBH4, the mix was refluxed for 2 h. The obtained CdTe QDs was precipitated with ethanol, centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 10 min), to remove the supernatant. They were redispersed in 40 mL water and stored at 4 °C.

The composites were assemblied by a modified process [22]. A solution of CdTe QDs (0.1 mmol) in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH = 7.4) were added with 0.12 mmol EDC and 0.08 mmol NHS under stirring over 0.5 h. Then, 0.2 mmol APBA was added to form an amide bond between the QDs and APBA. Finally, 100 μL NaOH was used to adjust the pH to 10.4, and 0.04 mmol γ-CD (dissolved in 0.1 mol/L PBS, pH = 10.4) was added to obtain γ-CD@ CdTe QDs solution, which was collected and centrifuged to achieve the sediment (15000 rpm, 10 min). Then, the product was washed alternately with deionized water and ethanol for three times. Afterwards, 20 μL ethanol solution of RBD (30 mg) was mixed with 15 mL aqueous solution of as-prepared γ-CD@ CdTe (100 mg). The mixture was stirred overnight at room temperature to obtain CdTe@γ-CD@RBD composite. Free RBD was removed by centrifugation at 15000 rpm for 10 min and washing with deionized water and ethanol for three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ and interference experiment
ce:section.ce:para: 5 mg of the probe in 10 mL of solution (the proportion of ethanol and water is 1:9 v/v) was provided. 100 μL of the probe solution was appended with various concentrations of Fe3+ (PBS, pH = 7.4). After incubation for 10 min, within a wavelength range between 450 and 700 nm, their fluorescence spectra were recorded. Cd2+, K+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ were added respectively to detect the selectivity of the composite materials for common positive ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Fe3+ in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: The serum samples were gathered from healthy people in the age of 18–48 years old and then blended with acetonitrile (ratio of 1:1, v: v). The solution was centrifuged (3500 rpm, 9 min) and the upper part was diluted 40-fold with PBS solution (pH = 7.4). During the fluorescence spectra study, the volume (human serum sample) kept constant while different concentrations of Fe3+ varied.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the CdTe QDs, RBD and the composites
ce:section.ce:para: The sketchy assembly process of CdTe@γ-CD@RBD composite was shown in Scheme 2 . To better determine the morphology and dispersion of the as-prepared CdTe QDs, we performed TEM and HRTEM characterizations, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The CdTe nanoparticles were spherical and uniformly dispersed, with an average particle size of about 3.7 nm. The HRTEM image inside in Fig. 1a shows that the QDs have clear lattice fringes with good crystalline properties. To further know the morphology and dispersion of the QDs, we performed its SEM characterization, as shown in Fig. 1c. The QDs were dispersed in ethanol, and the image was observed after sonicating for 2 min. It can be found that the nanoparticles are still spherical with relatively uniform diameter and a slight agglomeration phenomenon in the ethanol solution.

The TEM image of CdTe@γ-CD@RBD composites has a relatively uniform distribution with an average particle size of about 4.5 nm, and the dispersion is very well (Fig. 1b). The diameter is significantly larger, and the QDs become slightly non-standard spherical after assembly, indicating that the composites have been possibly formed. Similarly, the HRTEM diagram embedded in Fig. 2 b shows that the QDs based composites have clear lattice fringes and good crystallization performance.

The crystal phase of the QDs can also be characterized by XRD (Fig. 1d). The result shows that it has three distinct diffraction peaks at 24.7°, 41.7° and 49.6°, indicating the synthesized QDs have good crystalline properties. It is confirmed that the three diffraction peaks are correspond to the (111), (220) and (311) crystal planes, respectively, belonging to the sphalerite cubic system and the face-centered cubic lattice (JCPDS 15–0770) [21]. The positions of the diffraction peaks have minor alterations compared to the standard data, implying a problem of preferred orientation in the growth of the nanocrystals [23].

The assembling process was also confirmed by FT-IR spectra (Fig. 2). The peaks of RBD at 2974, 1698 and 1509 cm−1 could confirm the formation of the RBD molecule [8]. The absorption peak of CdTe QDs at 1406 and 1571 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of carbonyl group. The absorption peak at 2926 cm−1 is –CH stretching vibration. There is no absorption peak at 2500–2600 cm−1, indicating that MPA molecule has been coordinated on the surface of QDs, which is used to stabilize the nanoparticles and prevent their agglomeration [24].

Theγ-CD is connected by the MPA via the secondary vicinal hydroxyl groups of the sugar units [25]. The FT-IR spectra of CdTe-γ-CD or γ-CD at 1160 (1167) cm−1 are in accordance with the asymmetric glycosidic vibration υa(COC) and at 1095 (1096) cm−1 conform to the coupled stretch vibration υa(CC/CO). The band at 3407 (3401) cm−1 comes from the OH vibrations and the band at 877 (873) cm−1 corresponds to the out-of-plane bending vibrationγ(CH) [22]. They confirm that γ-CD has been linked to the surface of the QDs. Meanwhile, CdTe@γ-CD and CdTe@γ-CD @RBD have characteristic absorption peaks near 1663 cm−1 and 1698 cm−1, which proves that the surface has a citrate trisodium structure. Therefore, it can be seen from the infrared spectrum that CdTe@γ-CD, CdTe@γ-CD@RBD, and RBD materials contain group information of their respective organic compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimize the amounts of γ-CD and RBD
ce:section.ce:para: The amount of γ-CD added in the synthesis experiment is an important factor for Fe3+ determination, because only the RBD that entered the cavity of γ-CD could induce FRET process. Then, the effect of γ-CD amount on the FRET process was studied. In the preparation process of CdTe@γ-CD@RBD, the other amounts were all fixed and the mole number of γ-CD was changed to get different CdTe@γ-CD@RBD. 0.5 mg mL−1 composites were added with 50 μM Fe3+ and the fluorescence quenching degree (I0-I)/I0 at 600 nm was tested, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3 a. It can be seen that the (I0-I)/I0 increased with the increasing γ-CD up to 0.04 mmol. This could attribute to the increasing amount γ-CD can accommodate more RBD to induce more FRET between QDs and RBD-Fe3+. When the amount of γ-CD was higher than 0.04 mmol, the (I0-I)/I0 decreased. This phenomena could attribute to the reason that a part of RBD molecular could enter the cavities of dissociative γ-CD by hydrophobic interaction when excess γ-CD exist in solution, so the amount of RBD enter the cavities of γ-CD-functionalized QDs would decrease, which resulted in less FRET between QDs and RBD-Fe3+. Therefore, the mole number of γ-CD of 0.04 mmol was selected for further experiments.

In the same way, we studied the optimal loading amount for RBD. In the preparation process of CdTe@γ-CD@RBD, the other amounts were all fixed and the mass of RBD was changed to get different CdTe@γ-CD@RBD composites. 0.5 mg mL−1 probe solution were added with 50 μM Fe3+ and the fluorescence quenching degree (I0-I)/I0 at 600 nm was tested, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3b. It shows that the (I0-I)/I0 increased with the increasing RBD up to 30 mg. This could ascribe to the increasing amount of RBD in the composite with the increasing RBD added in the experiment, which can lead to more FRET between QDs and RBD-Fe3+. When the amount of RBD was higher than 30 mg, the (I0-I)/I0 had little change. It is the reason that the optimal loading amount for RBD is 30 mg, excess RBD was washed off by the purification. Therefore, the mass of RBD of 30 mg was selected for the experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The FRET activity of the probe
ce:section.ce:para: In order to better evaluate the FRET activity of the nanoprobe, the UV–vis spectra of RBD before and after addition of Fe3+ and the fluorescence spectrum of the QDs are shown in Fig. 4 a. No absorption peak appears in RBD solution when Fe3+ was not added. However, after the addition of Fe3+, a wider characteristic absorption appears at 559 nm, indicating a strong interaction between RBD and Fe3+. This is because of the loop-opening mechanism, which is the reaction caused by the chelation of Fe3+ with oxygen atoms on the amide group of RBD [26]. At the excitation light of 290 nm, the QDs have an obvious emission peak at 600 nm. The UV–vis absorption intensity of RBD-Fe3+ can partially overlap with the fluorescence emission peak of the QDs. Therefore, there is a possibility of FRET occurrence, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence emission intensity of the composites. The FRET efficiency between the QDs and RBD can be adjusted by the concentration of Fe3+ to realize its detection.

Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetimes of the CdTe@γ-CD@RBD solution in the absence and presence of Fe3+ were measured. As shown in Fig. 4b, the composite shows three exponential decay with the longest fluorescence lifetime of 22.7 ns. However, after adding Fe3+, the fluorescence lifetime was decreased with two exponential decay to 4.4 ns, further demonstrating there is a FRET process from QDs to RBD-Fe3+ [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity and selectivity of CdTe@γ-CD@RBD to Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the probe with different concentrations of Fe3+ were tested. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the intensity gradually increases with the augment of Fe3+ concentration. At the same time, the intensity and Fe3+ concentration form a good linear relationship and the linear equation is A = 1.191 + 0.0096CFe 3+ (R2 = 0.9949) (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence emission intensity of the probe with Fe3+ was also studied under different concentrations of Fe3+ (Fig. 5c). An excellent linear relationship is likewise formed between the napierian logarithm of degree of fluorescence quenching and the concentration of Fe3+ between 10 and 60 μM (Fig. 5d). The linear equation is Ln(I-I0) = 3.252 + 0.0227CFe 3+ (R2 = 0.9965), with a calculated detection limit of 2.51 μM and a quenching constant of 0.0227. Obviously, there is an adjustable FRET efficiency between fluorescent probe and Fe3+ according to the addition of different amounts of Fe3+. Therefore, the composites showed high sensitivity in Fe3+ sensing.

In order to detect the influence of various ions on the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission of composite materials, interfering ions experiments were carried out and the results were shown in Fig. 6 . 250 μM interfering ions such as a mixture of metal cations (Cd2+, K+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Na+, Pb2+), Hg2+and 50 μM Fe3+ were added respectively to the composite material solution for test [28]. When other ions（except Fe3+）were added to the composites, no obvious UV–visible absorption peak appeared. When Fe3+ was added, the UV–vis absorption intensity enhanced significantly at 559 nm.

For the fluorescence spectra, when Fe3+ was added to the CdTe@γ-CD@RBD solution, the intensity decreased significantly, while it almost did not change when other ions were added. As can be seen from Fig. 6b, no matter whether other ions exist or not, interfering ions have little influence on fluorescence intensity and basically remain unchanged. It shows that the QDs composite materials can be used to detect Fe3+ in practical samples.

The above-mentioned results were explained in Scheme 3 , which can be attributed to the spiroring opening mechanism of the RBD molecule. More specifically, the chelation of Fe3+ with the oxygen atoms of the amide groups of RBD results in the formation of the open-ring form. The stability constant of other metal ions binding to RBD is too low to be detected while the value with Fe3+ is as high as 103.5 [26]. When Fe3+ was added, the UV–vis absorption peak of RBD at 559 nm was enhanced, which overlapped with the fluorescence emission spectrum of the QDs, resulting in FRET effect.

Finally, the reliability and potentials of the proposed sensor were appraised with actual biological samples under optimal conditions (all experiments were operated with three times). Human serum was routinely pretreated, diluted with PBS solution, and spiked with known concentrations of Fe3+ [29]. The results for Fe3+ detecting are listed in Table 1 . Recoveries in human serum range from 98.60 to 107.20 % with RSD of around 1.43 %-2.96 %. The obtained RSD values indicate excellent accuracy and good potentials for sensing Fe3+ in practical biological samples.

In order to evaluate the effectiveness of this detection method, comparisons were done with the previously method for Fe3+ sensing. Table 2 shows a comparison of each LOD (limit of detection) and linear range. In terms of effectiveness and reliability, this method has a moderate linear range compared with other methods previously reported.

In summary, a fluorescence sensing probe for Fe3+ detection was designed by combining CdTe QDs with γ -CD and then assembled with RBD. The QDs was used as the energy donor and RBD as the energy receptor. With the increase of Fe3+ concentration, the UV–vis absorption intensity of RBD-Fe3+ increases, and there is a FRET possibility with QDs, which leads to a decrease in fluorescence emission intensity of composite materials for the detection of Fe3+. Since a lot of hydrophobic units can also be bagged into the pocket of γ -CD, the self-assembly can not only serve as a versatile platform for other sensing, but also drive the hydrophobic probes to work in water. Therefore, the method can be extended to other fluorescent sensing systems.

Wenlu Wu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. Zhongfei Hu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation. Cai Shi: Data curation, Formal analysis. Ruoqian Xu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yiming Zhao: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yujie Ding: Methodology, Conceptualization, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the overseas visiting and research project for outstanding young talents of colleges and universities in Anhui province (gxgwfx2019043), undergraduate scientific research project of Anhui polytechnic university in 2022 (2022DZ25) and College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Fund in Anhui province (S201910363194 , S202110363275).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of multi-functional carbon dots based on “one stone, three birds” strategy and their applications for the dual-mode Fe3+ detection, effective promotion on cell proliferation and treatment on ferric toxicosis in vitro
J. Mater. Chem. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cai, H.; Xu, H.; Chu, H.; Li, J.; Zhang, D., Fabrication of multi-functional carbon dots based on “one stone, three birds” strategy and their applications for the dual-mode Fe3+ detection, effective promotion on cell proliferation and treatment on ferric toxicosis in vitro. Journal of Materials Chemistry B 2021, 9 (3), 767-782.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fe3+-Sensitive Carbon Dots for Detection of Fe3+ in Aqueous Solution and Intracellular Imaging of Fe3+ Inside Fungal Cells
Front. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, Y. Q.; Sun, X. B.; Pan, W.; Yu, G. F.; Wang, J. P., Fe3+-Sensitive Carbon Dots for Detection of Fe3+ in Aqueous Solution and Intracellular Imaging of Fe3+ Inside Fungal Cells. Frontiers in Chemistry 2020, 7, 911.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent cellulose nanocrystals based on AIE luminogen for rapid detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solutions
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ye, X.; Zhang, D. Y.; Wang, S.; Zhou, P.; Zhu, P. L., Fluorescent cellulose nanocrystals based on AIE luminogen for rapid detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solutions. Rsc Advances 2022, 12 (38), 24633-24639.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Blue-Emitting Ligand-Mediated Assembly of Rare-Earth MOFs toward White-Light Emission, Sensing, Magnetic, and Catalytic Studies
Inorg. Chem.

New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yang, B., Sensing and photocatalytic properties of a new 3D Co(II) coordination polymer based on 1,1 '-di(p-carboxybenzyl)-2,2 '-biimidazole. New Journal of Chemistry 2022, 46 (39), 18848-18853.
